name: azure-servicebus-py
description: Azure Service Bus SDK for Python messaging. Use for queues, topics, subscriptions, and enterprise messaging patterns.
capabilities: Send and receive messages using Azure Service Bus queues
Implement pub/sub patterns with topics and subscriptions
Handle message settlement with complete, abandon, and dead-letter actions
Use sessions for FIFO ordered message processing
Manage dead-letter queues for failed message processing
author: sickn33
securitySummary: Documentation-only skill containing Azure Service Bus SDK usage examples. Static analysis scanned 0 files (0 lines) and detected 0 potential security issues. This is a prompt-only skill with no executable code that could pose security risks.
valueStatement: Add reliable cloud messaging to your Python applications with Azure Service Bus queues and pub/sub topics for enterprise-grade message delivery.
userTitle: Use Azure Service Bus in Python
limitations: Does not include Azure subscription or Service Bus namespace provisioning
No built-in retry policies or circuit breaker patterns in skill
Does not cover advanced features like auto-forwarding or entity description

useCases.title: Cloud Application Messaging
useCases.description: Implement asynchronous communication between microservices using queues for reliable message delivery.

useCases.title: Event-Driven Architecture
useCases.description: Set up pub/sub patterns with topics and subscriptions for distributed system event handling.

useCases.targetUser: Integration engineers working with enterprise systems
useCases.title: Enterprise Integration
useCases.description: Handle high-volume message processing with sessions, batches, and dead-letter queues for robust integration.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Queue Send and Receive
promptTemplates.prompt: Show me how to send a message to an Azure Service Bus queue and receive it using the Python SDK with async client.
promptTemplates.scenario: Getting started with Service Bus messaging

promptTemplates.title: Topic Subscription Setup
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a Python script that sends messages to an Azure Service Bus topic and receives messages from a subscription.

promptTemplates.title: Message Settlement with Dead-Letter
promptTemplates.prompt: Write Python code that receives messages, processes them, completes successful messages, and moves failed messages to a dead-letter queue.
promptTemplates.scenario: Handling message failures and poison messages

promptTemplates.title: Session-Based FIFO Processing
promptTemplates.prompt: Show how to use Azure Service Bus sessions in Python to ensure FIFO ordering for related messages like order processing.

bestPractices: Use async client (ServiceBusClient) for production workloads to handle high concurrency
Always use context managers (async with) to ensure proper resource cleanup
Set max_wait_time on receive operations to prevent indefinite blocking
antiPatterns: Do not forget to complete messages after processing - unprocessed messages will remain locked
Avoid sending very large messages - Service Bus has a 256KB limit per message
Do not skip dead-letter queue monitoring - failed messages lose visibility without it

faq.question: How do I authenticate with Azure Service Bus?
faq.answer: Use azure-identity DefaultAzureCredential for managed identity or service principal authentication. The SDK supports Azure AD tokens for secure authentication.

faq.question: What is the difference between a queue and a topic?
faq.answer: Queues provide point-to-point messaging where one sender sends to one receiver. Topics enable pub/sub where one sender broadcasts to multiple subscriptions.

faq.question: How do I handle failed message processing?
faq.answer: Use abandon_message() to release the lock for retry, or dead_letter_message() to move the message to a dead-letter queue for investigation.

faq.question: Can I process messages in order?
faq.answer: Yes, use sessions (session_id) to ensure FIFO ordering. All messages with the same session_id are processed sequentially by a single receiver.

faq.question: What is the message size limit?
faq.answer: Azure Service Bus supports messages up to 256KB. For larger payloads, use Azure Blob Storage and send the blob URL in the message.

faq.question: How do I schedule a message for later delivery?
faq.answer: Use sender.schedule_messages() with a datetime to schedule. Use the returned sequence_number to cancel the scheduled message if needed.
